data.name: Classic Espresso
data.description: The foundation of all espresso drinks. A properly pulled espresso is a thing of beauty: a rich, syrupy body topped with a layer of golden crema. This recipe focuses on the standard double shot, the benchmark every home barista should master before moving on to more complex drinks.
technique: Purge the group head with a short flush of water
Grind 18g of fresh coffee directly into the portafilter
Use a WDT tool to break up any clumps and distribute evenly
Tamp with firm, level pressure (about 30 lbs / 15 kg)
Lock the portafilter into the group head
Start extraction immediately
Target 36g of liquid espresso in 25-30 seconds
The stream should look like warm honey dripping from the spout
Stop the shot when you reach your target yield
tips: If the shot runs too fast (under 20 seconds), grind finer
If the shot runs too slow (over 35 seconds), grind coarser
Always adjust grind in small increments
Fresh beans (7-21 days post roast) produce the best crema
Taste first, then adjust. Sour = under-extracted, bitter = over-extracted
Pre-heat your cup with hot water for better temperature retention
pairsWith: A small glass of sparkling water to cleanse the palate

data.name: Caffe Latte
data.description: The latte is the crowd-pleaser of the espresso world. A double shot of espresso combined with silky steamed milk creates a smooth, approachable drink that highlights the sweeter, more caramel notes in your coffee. Mastering the milk texture is the key to a great latte, both for taste and for latte art.
technique: Pull a double espresso (18g in, 36g out, 25-30 seconds) into your cup
While the shot pulls, begin steaming 200ml of cold whole milk
Position the steam wand tip just below the milk surface
Introduce air for the first 2-3 seconds (you should hear a gentle hissing)
Submerge the tip to create a whirlpool, heating to 60-65°C
Tap the pitcher on the counter to pop any large bubbles
Swirl the pitcher to integrate the foam into a glossy, paint-like texture
Pour the milk into the espresso from a moderate height, then bring the spout close to the surface for latte art
tips: Cold milk gives you more time to texture properly
Whole milk produces the best microfoam due to its fat and protein content
The finished milk should look like wet white paint with no visible bubbles
For latte art, the milk needs to be poured within 10 seconds of finishing steaming
A 350ml pitcher is ideal for a single latte
Oat milk is the best dairy alternative for latte art

data.name: Cappuccino
data.description: The traditional Italian cappuccino features equal parts espresso, steamed milk, and milk foam. Compared to a latte, the cappuccino has more air incorporated into the milk, creating a lighter, airier texture with a more prominent foam cap. In Italy, cappuccinos are exclusively a morning drink, never ordered after 11 AM.
data.yield: 36g espresso + 120ml steamed milk with thick foam
technique: Pull a double espresso (18g in, 36g out, 25-30 seconds) into a 180ml cappuccino cup
Steam 120ml of cold whole milk
Introduce more air than for a latte: keep the tip near the surface for 4-5 seconds
You want about 1cm of foam on top of the milk in the pitcher
Continue heating with a whirlpool motion until 60-65°C
Tap and swirl the pitcher aggressively to break up any large bubbles
Pour the milk into the center of the espresso with a slight wiggle
The foam should sit proud above the rim of the cup
tips: The key difference from a latte is more air and less liquid milk
A proper cappuccino foam should hold a teaspoon of sugar on its surface for a moment
Use a smaller cup than a latte to maintain the proper ratio
Traditional Italian cappuccinos are 150-180ml total
Chocolate powder on top is optional and not traditional in Italy
The foam should be silky, not dry or stiff

data.name: Flat White
data.description: Originating from Australia and New Zealand, the flat white is the espresso-forward milk drink. It uses a ristretto double shot (a shorter, more concentrated extraction) combined with velvety microfoam that has very little air. The result is a drink where the espresso character shines through the milk, rather than being diluted by it.
technique: Pull a ristretto double (18g in, 27g out, 20-25 seconds) into a 180ml cup
Steam 150ml of cold whole milk with minimal air incorporation
Only introduce air for 1-2 seconds, just enough for a thin microfoam layer
Heat the milk to no more than 60°C for sweetness
The finished milk should be glossy with almost no visible foam
Pour directly into the center of the ristretto
The thin microfoam creates a flat, glossy surface, hence the name
Finish with a small latte art pattern if desired
tips: The ristretto shot is key. It should be sweeter and more intense than a standard shot
Less foam is the defining characteristic. Aim for almost no foam at all
Use a smaller cup (160-180ml) to keep the espresso-to-milk ratio strong
The lower milk temperature lets the coffee flavor come through
A flat white should taste noticeably more coffee-forward than a latte
This drink really rewards using high-quality, fresh beans

data.name: Pour-Over Coffee
data.description: Pour-over coffee is a meditative brewing method that gives you complete control over every variable. The slow, deliberate pouring of water over a bed of coffee produces a remarkably clean, nuanced cup that highlights the unique characteristics of single-origin beans. This recipe uses a standard V60-style dripper.
technique: Boil water and let it cool to 92-96°C (or use immediately off boil for light roasts)
Place a paper filter in the dripper and rinse thoroughly with hot water
Discard the rinse water from your server
Add 15g of medium-fine ground coffee to the filter
Bloom: pour 30-45g of water in a spiral, wetting all grounds evenly
Wait 30-45 seconds for the bloom (you should see bubbles forming as CO2 escapes)
First pour: slowly add water in concentric circles to 150g total (30 seconds)
Wait until the water level drops near the coffee bed
Second pour: add water to 250g total in concentric circles
Allow all water to draw through. Total time should be 2:30-3:30
tips: A gooseneck kettle is essential for controlling your pour rate and placement
Always rinse the paper filter to remove papery taste and preheat the dripper
Stale coffee will not bloom visibly. Use beans within 4 weeks of roasting
Adjust grind size based on total brew time: too fast means grind finer, too slow means grind coarser
Pour in slow, steady concentric circles, avoiding the edges of the filter
Light roast single-origin coffees tend to shine best with this method
The Arco Filtro grinder is optimized for pour-over grind consistency

data.name: Cold Brew Coffee
data.description: Cold brew coffee is steeped overnight in cold water, producing a smooth, low-acidity concentrate that is perfect for hot summer days. The long, slow extraction at room temperature or below draws out the sweeter, chocolatey notes while leaving behind much of the bitterness. Makes a concentrate that can be diluted to taste.
technique: Grind 100g of coffee on the coarsest setting
Add the ground coffee to a large jar or cold brew maker
Pour 500ml of filtered room-temperature water over the grounds
Stir gently to ensure all grounds are saturated
Cover and place in the refrigerator (or leave at room temperature for a bolder result)
Steep for 12-24 hours (16 hours is the sweet spot for most people)
Strain through a fine mesh filter, then through a paper filter for clarity
Store the concentrate in the fridge for up to 2 weeks
To serve, dilute 1:1 with cold water, milk, or pour over ice
requiredEquipment: Large jar or cold brew maker
tips: Use a medium to dark roast for the best cold brew flavor profile
A coarse grind prevents over-extraction during the long steep time
Refrigerator steeping produces a cleaner, milder result. Room temperature is bolder
The concentrate is strong. Always dilute before drinking
Cold brew naturally has about 60% less acidity than hot-brewed coffee
Add a pinch of salt to the concentrate to enhance sweetness and reduce bitterness
Try serving over coffee ice cubes (frozen cold brew) to prevent dilution

data.name: Americano
data.description: The Americano bridges the gap between espresso and drip coffee. By adding hot water to a double espresso, you get a long, sippable drink that retains the flavor complexity of espresso with a lighter, more approachable body. Legend has it that American soldiers in Italy during World War II diluted espresso to resemble the drip coffee they were accustomed to.
technique: Pull a double espresso (18g in, 36g out, 25-30 seconds)
Heat 150-200ml of fresh water to 80-85°C (slightly below boiling to avoid scalding the espresso)
For a traditional Americano: add the hot water to the cup first, then pour the espresso on top. This preserves the crema
For a Long Black (Australian style): pour espresso directly into the hot water
Adjust the water amount to your preferred strength
Serve immediately
tips: The water temperature matters. Too hot and it scorches the espresso. 80-85°C is ideal
Adding espresso to water (Long Black style) preserves more crema on top
Use slightly less coffee than normal drip to avoid an overly intense flavor
An Americano from a good shot reveals flavors you might miss in straight espresso
Many machines have a hot water spout that can be used directly
This is a great drink for showcasing lighter roasted single-origin espressos

data.name: Cortado
data.description: The cortado is the perfect balance of espresso and milk. Originating from Spain (cortar means to cut), a cortado cuts the intensity of espresso with an equal measure of warm, lightly steamed milk. There is minimal foam, the milk temperature is lower than a latte, and the drink is served in a small 120-150ml glass. It is pure simplicity.
technique: Pull a double espresso (18g in, 36g out, 25-30 seconds) into a small glass
Steam 60ml of milk just until warm (55-60°C), with almost no foam
Alternatively, heat milk gently in a saucepan without frothing
Pour the warm milk directly into the espresso
The result should be a 1:1 ratio of espresso to milk
Serve in a small cortado glass or tumbler
tips: The cortado is about simplicity. Do not overthink the milk
The milk should be warm, not hot. This keeps the drink drinkable in a few sips
There should be virtually no foam. Just warm, flat milk
This is an excellent drink for tasting different espresso beans
In many Spanish cafes, the cortado is the default coffee order
The Arco Double-Wall Glasses are perfect for cortados

data.name: Espresso Macchiato
data.description: The macchiato (meaning stained or spotted in Italian) is an espresso marked with just a small dollop of milk foam. It is the purists compromise: nearly a straight espresso, but with just enough milk to soften the edge. Do not confuse this with the sugary, flavored drinks that some chains have turned the name into. A true macchiato is just espresso with a spot of foam.
technique: Pull a double espresso (18g in, 36g out, 25-30 seconds) into a small espresso cup
Steam a small amount of milk (50ml) to create a thick, dense foam
Using a spoon, place a single dollop of foam on top of the espresso
The foam should float on the crema, creating a white spot on the brown surface
Serve immediately in an espresso cup
tips: The macchiato is 95% espresso, 5% milk. It should taste like espresso
Use a small spoon to place the foam precisely
Some baristas use a tiny pitcher to pour a dot of steamed milk instead
This is traditionally an afternoon drink in Italy
If you want slightly more milk, you are making a cortado instead
The foam acts as an insulator, keeping the espresso warm slightly longer

data.name: Affogato
data.description: Is it coffee? Is it dessert? The affogato (meaning drowned in Italian) is gloriously both. A scoop of cold vanilla gelato is drowned in a shot of hot espresso, creating a stunning contrast of temperatures, textures, and flavors. The espresso melts the gelato slowly, evolving from a frozen treat into a rich, creamy coffee drink. Pure Italian genius.
technique: Place a scoop of high-quality vanilla gelato or ice cream into a chilled glass or bowl
Pull a double espresso (18g in, 36g out, 25-30 seconds)
Immediately pour the hot espresso directly over the gelato
Serve immediately with a spoon
Eat and drink as it melts, enjoying the evolving flavors and temperatures
tips: Quality gelato or ice cream makes all the difference. Use the best you can find
Vanilla is traditional, but hazelnut and pistachio gelato are incredible alternatives
A small glass keeps the portions in balance. Too much gelato overwhelms the espresso
Serve immediately. The beauty is in the contrast of hot and cold
For an adults-only version, add a splash of Amaretto or Frangelico
Use a slightly longer (standard ratio) shot rather than a ristretto for better balance with the sweet gelato

data.name: Caffe Mocha
data.description: The mocha is the indulgent crossover where coffee meets chocolate. A double espresso is combined with rich chocolate sauce and steamed milk, creating a drink that satisfies both coffee cravings and sweet tooth. Topped with whipped cream or chocolate shavings, the mocha is comfort in a cup and a gateway drink for those new to espresso-based beverages.
technique: Add 20g of high-quality chocolate sauce or melted dark chocolate to the bottom of a 240ml cup
Pull a double espresso (18g in, 36g out, 25-30 seconds) directly onto the chocolate
Stir vigorously to fully incorporate the chocolate into the espresso
Steam 150ml of cold whole milk to 60-65°C with a latte-like microfoam
Pour the steamed milk over the chocolate-espresso mixture
Optionally top with whipped cream and chocolate shavings or cocoa powder
Serve immediately
tips: Use real chocolate or high-quality chocolate sauce, not chocolate syrup, for the best flavor
70% dark chocolate provides the best balance with espresso
Stir the chocolate and espresso thoroughly before adding milk to avoid clumps at the bottom
For a white mocha, use white chocolate sauce instead
A mocha is an excellent choice for introducing espresso to people who find it too intense
For an iced mocha, use cold milk and pour everything over ice instead of steaming

data.name: Iced Latte
data.description: A refreshing iced latte combines the richness of espresso with the smooth coolness of cold milk over ice. The key to a great iced latte is pulling a concentrated, slightly shorter espresso that can stand up to dilution from the ice, and using enough ice to chill the drink instantly without watering it down over time.
technique: Fill a tall glass (350-400ml) to the top with ice
Pour 200ml of cold milk over the ice
Pull a double espresso (18g in, 36g out, 25-30 seconds)
Slowly pour the hot espresso over the milk and ice
The espresso will cool instantly and create beautiful layers
Stir to combine, or serve layered for visual effect
Optional: add simple syrup or flavored syrup before the milk
tips: Use plenty of ice. The espresso needs to be chilled rapidly to avoid a lukewarm drink
Cold milk poured first prevents the ice from melting too quickly
For a creamier result, use cold whole milk or oat milk
Pouring espresso over ice first (flash chilling) then adding milk is an alternative method
Coffee ice cubes prevent dilution as they melt
A slightly finer grind can compensate for the dilution from ice

data.name: Ristretto
data.description: The ristretto is espresso distilled to its purest essence. By using the same dose of coffee but pulling only half the water through, you get a shorter, more concentrated shot that emphasizes sweetness and body while leaving behind the bitter compounds that extract later. It is the Italian word for restricted, and the restriction is what makes it special.
technique: Grind 18g of fresh coffee slightly finer than your standard espresso setting
Distribute and tamp as normal with firm, level pressure
Lock the portafilter and start extraction
Stop the shot at 18-22g of liquid, before the stream turns blonde
The extraction should take 20-25 seconds
The shot should be thick, syrupy, and intensely aromatic
Serve immediately in a pre-warmed demitasse
tips: The finer grind compensates for the reduced water volume
A ristretto should taste noticeably sweeter than a standard espresso
Stop the shot before the stream begins to pale and thin out
This is the base shot used in flat whites at many specialty cafes
If the shot tastes sour, your grind may be too coarse for the reduced volume
Try single-origin medium roasts to appreciate the concentrated flavor nuances
pairsWith: A single piece of fine chocolate

data.name: Lungo
data.description: The lungo (Italian for long) is the opposite of a ristretto. More water is pushed through the same dose of coffee, producing a larger, less concentrated shot. The extended extraction pulls out more of the bean's complexity, including some of the pleasant bitter notes that add depth. Think of it as espresso with more room to breathe.
technique: Grind 18g of coffee slightly coarser than your standard espresso setting
Distribute and tamp with firm, level pressure
Lock the portafilter and start extraction
Allow the shot to run until you reach 54-60g of liquid
The total extraction time should be 35-45 seconds
The stream will start rich and darken, then gradually turn lighter
Serve in a slightly larger cup than a standard espresso
tips: Grind slightly coarser to avoid over-extraction with the longer contact time
A lungo is not the same as an Americano. The water passes through the coffee, not around it
Medium to dark roasts work best as they can handle the extended extraction without turning harsh
If the lungo tastes overly bitter, coarsen the grind or shorten the extraction slightly
This is a popular morning drink in Northern Europe
The Arco Primo and Doppio both have programmable lungo presets

data.name: Espresso Romano
data.description: The Espresso Romano pairs a straight double shot with a twist of fresh lemon peel. The citrus oils brighten the espresso and cut through the intensity, creating a surprisingly refreshing combination. Despite the name, it is not particularly common in Rome itself, but it has become a beloved ritual in espresso bars around the world.
technique: Cut a thin strip of peel from an unwaxed lemon, avoiding the white pith
Twist the peel over a pre-warmed espresso cup to release the oils
Rub the peel around the rim of the cup
Pull a double espresso (18g in, 36g out, 25-30 seconds) into the cup
Place the lemon peel on the saucer alongside the cup
Sip the espresso, alternating with small bites or sniffs of the peel
tips: Use only the outer yellow peel. The white pith is bitter and will ruin the effect
Unwaxed organic lemons have the strongest, cleanest citrus oils
The lemon should complement, not overpower. Do not squeeze juice into the espresso
Medium roasts with fruity or floral notes pair best with the citrus
Some prefer to drop the peel into the cup and let it infuse as they drink
Try it with a Meyer lemon for an even sweeter, more fragrant twist

data.name: Cafe Cubano
data.description: The Cafe Cubano is Miami in a cup. Dark, sweet, and intensely caffeinated. Demerara sugar is whipped with the first drops of espresso to create espumita, a thick, caramel-colored foam that sits atop the finished shot. The result is a drink that is simultaneously bitter, sweet, and powerfully aromatic. It is traditionally shared from a single brew into small tacitas.
technique: Add 2 teaspoons of demerara or raw sugar to a small metal pitcher or cup
Start pulling a double espresso
As soon as the first few drops emerge, divert them into the sugar
Whip the sugar and espresso drops vigorously with a spoon until a thick, pale caramel paste forms. This is the espumita
Let the rest of the espresso finish pulling normally
Pour the remaining espresso into the espumita
Stir briefly. The espumita should rise to the top as a sweet, creamy foam
Pour into small demitasse cups (tacitas) to share
tips: The espumita is everything. Whip it aggressively until it turns light caramel and thickens
Demerara sugar works better than white sugar because it caramelizes more richly
Use a dark roast for the most authentic flavor
In Miami, a Cubano is the default coffee order. Just say cafe
The communal aspect is part of the ritual. Make a full pot and share with friends
If the espumita is not forming, add one more drop of espresso and keep whipping

data.name: Piccolo Latte
data.description: The piccolo latte is the barista's secret weapon: a single ristretto shot topped with silky steamed milk in a small 90-120ml glass. Originally created so baristas could taste-test espresso all day without overdosing on caffeine and milk, the piccolo has become a menu staple for those who want espresso character with just a touch of dairy softness.
technique: Pull a single ristretto (18g in, 18-22g out, 20-25 seconds) into a small latte glass
Steam 80ml of cold whole milk with light microfoam, similar to a flat white texture
Introduce air for only 1-2 seconds at the start
Heat the milk to 60°C for maximum sweetness
Pour the milk over the ristretto in one smooth motion
The drink should fill a 90-120ml glass
Latte art is optional but a nice touch on such a small canvas
tips: The ristretto base is what makes this different from a small latte
Keep the milk volume low. A piccolo should be 3-4 sips, not a long drink
This is ideal for mid-morning when you want something milky but not heavy
Baristas often use piccolos to dial in new beans throughout the day
The small format means bean quality really matters. Use fresh, high-quality coffee
Oat milk also works beautifully in a piccolo

data.name: Dirty Chai Latte
data.description: The dirty chai latte combines the warm, spiced complexity of masala chai with the bold kick of espresso. The espresso shot dirties the chai, adding a roasted depth that plays beautifully against the cinnamon, cardamom, and ginger. It is the best of both worlds for those who cannot choose between tea and coffee.
technique: Prepare 100ml of strong chai concentrate (steep black tea with cinnamon, cardamom, ginger, cloves, and black pepper in water for 5 minutes)
Strain the chai into a large cup
Pull a double espresso (18g in, 36g out, 25-30 seconds)
Pour the espresso into the chai and stir
Steam 120ml of whole milk to 60-65°C with light microfoam
Pour the steamed milk over the chai-espresso mixture
Dust with a pinch of ground cinnamon
tips: Use a strong chai concentrate so the spices hold up against the espresso
Pre-made chai concentrates work in a pinch, but fresh is noticeably better
Adjust sweetness with honey or sugar to balance the bitter espresso
A double dirty uses two shots for a more coffee-forward drink
The spices pair best with medium to dark roast espresso
For an iced version, shake the chai and espresso with ice before adding cold milk

data.name: Oat Milk Cappuccino
data.description: The oat milk cappuccino has become the plant-based drink of choice for specialty coffee. Oat milk's natural sweetness, creamy body, and remarkable foaming ability make it the best dairy alternative for a cappuccino. The slightly cereal-like flavor of oats complements espresso in a way that no other plant milk can match, creating a drink that rivals its dairy counterpart.
technique: Pull a double espresso (18g in, 36g out, 25-30 seconds) into a cappuccino cup
Steam 120ml of barista-grade oat milk
Introduce air for 3-4 seconds to create a thick cappuccino foam
Continue heating with a whirlpool motion to 60-65°C
Oat milk can scorch easily, so monitor temperature carefully
Tap and swirl the pitcher to incorporate the foam
Pour with a gentle wiggle to create the cappuccino foam cap
tips: Use barista-edition oat milk. It is formulated to foam better and resist splitting
Oat milk foams slightly differently than dairy. Introduce air a touch longer
Do not overheat. Oat milk scorches above 70°C and develops an unpleasant taste
The natural sweetness of oat milk means you may not need added sugar
Medium roasts with chocolate or nutty notes complement oat milk beautifully
Shake the carton thoroughly before pouring. Oat milk separates when sitting

data.name: Spanish Latte
data.description: The Spanish latte, or cafe con leche condensada, swaps regular milk for sweetened condensed milk, creating a luxuriously rich, caramelized drink. Popular across Spain, Latin America, and Southeast Asia, it turns a standard latte into something closer to liquid dessert. The condensed milk's deep sweetness and thick texture pair with espresso in a way that regular sugar simply cannot replicate.
technique: Add 2 tablespoons (30ml) of sweetened condensed milk to the bottom of a cup
Pull a double espresso (18g in, 36g out, 25-30 seconds) directly onto the condensed milk
Stir vigorously to dissolve the condensed milk into the espresso
Steam 120ml of whole milk to 60-65°C with light microfoam
Pour the steamed milk over the espresso-condensed milk mixture
For an iced version, skip the steaming and pour cold milk over ice instead
tips: The condensed milk must be stirred into the espresso before adding fresh milk
Start with 2 tablespoons and adjust sweetness to your preference
This pairs especially well with bold, dark-roasted espresso
The iced version is incredibly popular in Southeast Asian coffee shops
For a lighter version, use evaporated milk instead of condensed
Canned condensed milk keeps well. Keep a tin in your coffee station

data.name: Iced Americano
data.description: The iced Americano is the no-nonsense summer coffee. A double shot of espresso poured over ice and topped with cold water produces a clean, strong, refreshing drink with zero added calories. It highlights the espresso's natural flavor without the sweetness of milk, making it perfect for those who prefer their coffee black but need it cold.
technique: Fill a tall glass (350ml) to the top with ice
Pour 150ml of cold filtered water over the ice
Pull a double espresso (18g in, 36g out, 25-30 seconds)
Pour the hot espresso directly over the ice and water
Stir gently to combine
The espresso will chill instantly on contact with the ice
tips: Use plenty of ice. The espresso needs to be shock-chilled to preserve flavor
Cold water first, then espresso. This prevents the ice from melting too fast
Sparkling water instead of still water makes a refreshing variation
A light or medium roast espresso can taste surprisingly fruity when iced
No sugar needed if the espresso is well-extracted. The cold brings out sweetness
Coffee ice cubes are a game-changer for avoiding dilution

data.name: Espresso Tonic
data.description: The espresso tonic is the cocktail-inspired drink that has taken specialty coffee by storm. A shot of espresso poured over ice-cold tonic water creates a sparkling, bittersweet drink with a stunning visual cascade as the espresso layers through the bubbles. It sounds unusual, but the quinine bitterness of tonic and the roasted bitterness of espresso complement each other perfectly.
technique: Fill a tall glass (300-350ml) with ice to the brim
Pour 150ml of premium tonic water over the ice
Optional: add a thin slice of lemon or orange
Pull a double espresso (18g in, 36g out, 25-30 seconds)
Very slowly pour the espresso over the back of a spoon onto the tonic
Watch the beautiful cascade as espresso layers through the tonic
Stir gently before drinking, or enjoy the layers as they naturally combine
tips: Use a high-quality tonic water. Cheap tonic is too sweet and ruins the balance
Fever-Tree or similar premium tonics with real quinine work best
Light and medium roast espressos with fruity or floral notes are ideal
The visual cascade is part of the experience. Use a clear glass and pour slowly
A citrus garnish adds aroma without overpowering the drink
This is a perfect non-alcoholic alternative for aperitivo hour

data.name: Shakerato
data.description: The shakerato is Italian summer elegance. A double espresso is shaken vigorously with ice and a touch of sugar in a cocktail shaker, then strained into a chilled coupe or martini glass. The shaking creates a cold, frothy espresso with a beautiful crema-like foam on top. It is the drink that proves Italian coffee culture extends well beyond the winter months.
technique: Pull a double espresso (18g in, 36g out, 25-30 seconds)
Add 1 teaspoon of sugar to the hot espresso and stir to dissolve (optional)
Fill a cocktail shaker halfway with ice
Pour the espresso (with dissolved sugar) into the shaker
Shake vigorously for 15-20 seconds until the shaker is frost-cold
Strain into a chilled coupe glass or martini glass
The drink should have a thick, frothy foam on top
tips: Dissolve the sugar in the hot espresso before shaking, not after
Shake hard and fast. The foam is the whole point
Strain immediately to avoid over-dilution from melting ice
A chilled glass keeps the drink cold longer
Demerara sugar adds a richer, more caramel sweetness than white sugar
For a variation, add a splash of Amaretto before shaking

data.name: Cold Brew Espresso Concentrate
data.description: This espresso-grade cold brew concentrate is brewed at a higher ratio than standard cold brew, producing a thick, syrupy liquid that can be used as a base for iced lattes, cocktails, or diluted into a strong black coffee. Brewed with an espresso roast, it delivers the boldness of espresso with the smooth, low-acid character of cold extraction.
technique: Grind 150g of espresso-roast coffee on a coarse setting
Add the grounds to a large jar or cold brew maker
Pour 500ml of cold filtered water over the grounds
Stir thoroughly to ensure all grounds are saturated
Seal and refrigerate for 18-24 hours
Strain through a fine mesh sieve, then through a paper filter
Store the concentrate in a sealed bottle in the fridge
The concentrate is very strong. Dilute 1:2 with water for black coffee, or 1:1 with milk for lattes
requiredEquipment: Large jar or cold brew maker
tips: The higher coffee-to-water ratio (1:3.3 vs standard 1:5) makes this a true concentrate
Use an espresso roast for bold, chocolatey results that cut through milk and ice
The concentrate keeps for up to 10 days refrigerated
This is perfect as a base for iced lattes, saving you from pulling espresso shots
A splash of this concentrate also makes an excellent espresso martini base
Strain twice, first through mesh, then through paper, for a crystal-clear result
pairsWith: Vanilla ice cream (pour over for a cold brew affogato)

data.name: Vietnamese Iced Coffee
data.description: Ca phe sua da is Vietnam's gift to the coffee world. A strong, dark brew drips slowly through a traditional phin filter directly onto a generous layer of sweetened condensed milk, then poured over a tall glass of ice. The result is a powerful, sweet, creamy iced coffee that is unlike anything else. While the traditional method uses a phin, an espresso machine produces an equally authentic flavor.
technique: Add 2-3 tablespoons (30-45ml) of sweetened condensed milk to a heat-safe glass
Pull a double espresso (18g in, 36g out, 25-30 seconds) directly onto the condensed milk
Stir thoroughly until the condensed milk is fully dissolved
Fill a tall glass to the brim with ice
Pour the espresso-condensed milk mixture over the ice
Stir well and drink through a straw
tips: Use a dark, bold roast. Vietnamese coffee is traditionally very strong and robust
The condensed milk should be sweetened, not evaporated. This is essential
Stir the condensed milk and espresso aggressively. Undissolved condensed milk pools at the bottom
Vietnamese coffee shops use Robusta beans for extra caffeine and bitterness. A dark espresso roast approximates this
For a traditional method, use a Vietnamese phin filter with coarsely ground dark roast
This drink is meant to be intensely sweet. Do not reduce the condensed milk your first time

data.name: Iced Caramel Macchiato
data.description: The iced caramel macchiato layers vanilla-sweetened cold milk, ice, espresso, and a drizzle of caramel for a visually stunning, indulgent iced drink. Unlike a regular macchiato, the espresso is poured on top of the milk (marking it from above), creating beautiful brown-and-white layers. The caramel drizzle on top adds a finishing touch of buttery sweetness.
technique: Add 15ml (1 tablespoon) of vanilla syrup to a tall glass
Pour 200ml of cold milk into the glass and stir to combine
Fill the glass to the top with ice
Pull a double espresso (18g in, 36g out, 25-30 seconds)
Slowly pour the espresso over the back of a spoon onto the ice and milk
The espresso should layer on top, creating a gradient effect
Drizzle caramel sauce in a crosshatch pattern over the top
Stir before drinking, or sip through the layers
tips: Pour the espresso very slowly for the best layering effect
Make your own vanilla syrup: simmer equal parts sugar and water with a split vanilla bean
Caramel sauce, not caramel syrup. The thicker sauce stays visible on top
The layered look only lasts a minute. Take your photo quickly
Whole milk or oat milk produce the best layers due to their density
For a lighter version, use sugar-free vanilla syrup and skip the caramel

data.name: Espresso Martini
data.description: The espresso martini is the drink that keeps you up and keeps the party going. Invented in London in the 1980s, it combines fresh espresso with vodka, coffee liqueur, and simple syrup, shaken hard with ice to create a frothy, caffeinated cocktail. The key is using freshly pulled espresso for maximum crema and flavor. It is the drink that launched a thousand late nights.
technique: Pull a double espresso (18g in, 36g out, 25-30 seconds) and let it cool slightly for 1 minute
Fill a cocktail shaker with ice
Add 45ml vodka, 30ml coffee liqueur (Kahlua or Mr Black), 30ml fresh espresso, and 15ml simple syrup
Shake vigorously for 15-20 seconds until the shaker is frosted
Double-strain into a chilled coupe or martini glass
Garnish with three coffee beans on the foam
tips: Fresh espresso is non-negotiable. The natural oils create the signature foam
Shake harder and longer than you think. The foam is what makes this drink special
Mr Black coffee liqueur is preferred by bartenders for its cleaner, less sweet profile
The espresso should be slightly warm when shaking, not hot and not cold
Three coffee beans represent health, happiness, and prosperity
Double-straining catches any ice chips for a silky-smooth result
For a non-alcoholic version, substitute vodka with seedlip or a non-alcoholic spirit

data.name: Coffee Negroni Mocktail
data.description: This non-alcoholic twist on the classic Negroni replaces gin, Campari, and vermouth with espresso, tonic water, and orange bitters. The result is a sophisticated, bitter, aromatic drink with the visual depth of a Negroni but powered by coffee instead of spirits. It is the ideal aperitivo for those who prefer caffeine over alcohol.
technique: Pull a double espresso (18g in, 36g out, 25-30 seconds) and let it cool for 2 minutes
Fill an old-fashioned glass with a single large ice cube
Add 45ml cooled espresso to the glass
Add 45ml premium tonic water
Add 30ml fresh blood orange juice (or regular orange juice)
Add 2-3 dashes of orange bitters
Stir gently to combine
Garnish with a twist of orange peel
tips: A large ice cube melts slowly, preventing dilution during the aperitivo
Blood orange juice adds a striking ruby color and slightly bitter edge
Let the espresso cool fully before mixing to prevent the ice from melting immediately
The orange bitters are essential. They bridge the espresso and citrus flavors
Serve before dinner as a non-alcoholic aperitivo
For a more bitter profile, add a splash of non-alcoholic Campari alternative

data.name: Pumpkin Spice Latte
data.description: Forget the chain-store version. A proper pumpkin spice latte uses real pumpkin puree and freshly ground spices combined with a strong espresso and steamed milk. The real pumpkin adds a velvety body and earthy sweetness that powdered syrup cannot match. This is autumn in a cup, the way it should be made.
technique: In a small saucepan, warm 2 tablespoons of pumpkin puree with 1 tablespoon of maple syrup
Add a pinch each of ground cinnamon, nutmeg, ginger, and cloves
Stir until smooth and fragrant, about 1 minute
Pull a double espresso (18g in, 36g out, 25-30 seconds) into the pumpkin mixture
Stir vigorously to incorporate
Steam 150ml of whole milk to 60-65°C with light microfoam
Pour the steamed milk over the pumpkin-espresso base
Top with a light dusting of cinnamon and nutmeg
tips: Use real pumpkin puree, not pumpkin pie filling, which has added sugar and spices
Canned pumpkin puree works perfectly. No need to roast a pumpkin
Maple syrup adds a richer sweetness than white sugar. Honey is a good alternative
Make a batch of pumpkin spice sauce and store in the fridge for up to a week
Freshly grated nutmeg on top makes a noticeable difference
Oat milk is an excellent dairy-free option for this recipe

data.name: Gingerbread Latte
data.description: The gingerbread latte is the taste of the winter holidays. A homemade gingerbread syrup made with fresh ginger, molasses, and warm spices is stirred into espresso and topped with steamed milk and a gingerbread cookie crumble. It is cozy, festive, and infinitely better than anything from a green-logoed chain.
technique: Make gingerbread syrup: simmer 100g brown sugar, 100ml water, 1 tsp ground ginger, 1/2 tsp cinnamon, 1/4 tsp nutmeg, 1/4 tsp cloves, and 1 tbsp molasses for 5 minutes. Strain and cool
Add 30ml of gingerbread syrup to a large mug
Pull a double espresso (18g in, 36g out, 25-30 seconds) into the mug
Stir to combine the syrup and espresso
Steam 150ml of whole milk to 60-65°C with light microfoam
Pour the steamed milk over the gingerbread espresso
Top with whipped cream and a sprinkle of ground cinnamon
Optionally crumble a small gingerbread cookie on top
tips: The homemade syrup is key and keeps in the fridge for up to 2 weeks
Molasses gives it the authentic dark gingerbread flavor. Do not skip it
Brown sugar is better than white for this recipe due to its caramel notes
Make a large batch of syrup at the start of December for the whole holiday season
A pinch of black pepper in the syrup adds a subtle warmth
This pairs beautifully with a medium-dark roast espresso

data.name: Lavender Honey Latte
data.description: The lavender honey latte is a floral, aromatic drink that bridges the gap between winter comfort and spring freshness. Dried culinary lavender is infused into honey to create a fragrant sweetener that pairs surprisingly well with espresso. The key is restraint: lavender should whisper, not shout. When done right, it is one of the most elegant flavored lattes you can make at home.
technique: Make lavender honey: gently warm 100g honey with 1 tablespoon dried culinary lavender for 5 minutes. Do not boil. Strain out the lavender buds
Add 15ml (1 tablespoon) of lavender honey to a cup
Pull a double espresso (18g in, 36g out, 25-30 seconds) into the cup
Stir to dissolve the honey into the espresso
Steam 180ml of whole milk to 60-65°C with a latte-like microfoam
Pour the steamed milk over the lavender-espresso
Garnish with a few dried lavender buds on the foam
tips: Use culinary-grade lavender only. Decorative lavender may have been treated with chemicals
Less is more with lavender. Too much makes the drink taste like soap
The infused honey keeps for months in a sealed jar at room temperature
Light roast espressos with floral or fruity notes complement lavender beautifully
An iced version works wonderfully in spring and early summer
Oat milk enhances the floral notes more than dairy in some people's experience
